sections.content: Ecclesiastical Latin has five vowels, just like English. The good news: they are simpler and more consistent than English vowels. Each vowel has one sound, and that sound never changes regardless of the word.

sections.title: The Five Vowels
sections.content:
A — always 'ah' as in 'father.' Never 'ay' as in 'cake.'

E — always 'eh' as in 'met.' Never 'ee' as in 'me.'

I — always 'ee' as in 'machine.' Never 'eye' as in 'mine.'

O — always 'oh' as in 'more.' Never 'uh' as in 'come.'

U — always 'oo' as in 'rule.' Never 'uh' as in 'cut.'
Think of Italian vowels — they are identical. If you can say 'pizza' and 'pasta' with an Italian accent, you already know how to pronounce Latin vowels.

sections.title: The Critical Difference: AE and OE
sections.content:
This is the single most important rule that separates Ecclesiastical from Classical Latin.

AE — pronounced 'ay' as in 'they.' NOT 'eye' as in Classical Latin.
Example: 'cælum' (heaven) = 'CHAY-loom,' not 'KYE-loom.'
Example: 'præsidium' = 'pray-SEE-dee-oom.'

OE — also pronounced 'ay' as in 'they.'
Example: 'pœnitentia' (penance) = 'pay-nee-TEN-tsee-ah.'

If you learn nothing else from this course, learn this: AE and OE say 'ay.' This one rule will immediately make your Latin sound like Church Latin rather than classroom Latin.
Five vowels, five sounds, no exceptions: A=ah, E=eh, I=ee, O=oh, U=oo. AE and OE both say 'ay.' Master these and you have the foundation for everything that follows.
